active_paradigms.idea: Gompertz double-exponential with x1 linear midpoint shift: y = c0*exp(-exp(c1 - c2*x0 - c3*x1)) + c4
active_paradigms.description: Replace the Richards generalized logistic with a Gompertz model y = c0*exp(-exp(c1 - c2*x0 - c3*x1)) + c4. The Gompertz naturally places its inflection point at ~37% of maximum amplitude (vs 50% for logistic), which matches the asymmetric sigmoidal shape of nucleation-elongation aggregation kinetics with extended lag phases. This uses only 5 constants vs 6 in Richards, reducing complexity and improving parsimony. The double-exponential structure is numerically stable for all real inputs. x1 shifts the midpoint linearly (same as current approach). Initial values: c0=1.0 (amplitude), c1=3.0 (midpoint control), c2=1e-3 (time rate), c3=0.001 (x1 shift), c4=0.0 (baseline). The Gompertz is a well-established model for amyloid aggregation kinetics and may provide better fits on datasets with pronounced lag phases.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: mean validation NMSE across all 61 datasets, parsimony via fewer constants
active_paradigms.cautions: The inner exponent c1 - c2*x0 - c3*x1 must remain finite; for large x0 it becomes very negative making exp() near zero which is fine. Initial c1 should be chosen so the midpoint falls within the observed time range. The outer exp(-exp(...)) is always in (0,1) so c0 controls amplitude and c4 the baseline.

active_paradigms.idea: Richards with x1 entering as rate multiplier via exp: y = c0/(1 + exp(c1 - c2*x0)*exp(-c3*x1))^c4 + c5
active_paradigms.description: Modify the current Richards model so x1 modulates the rate exponentially rather than linearly: y = c0/(1 + exp(c1 - c2*x0 - c3*x1))^c4 + c5. This is algebraically equivalent to the current form (since exp(c1 - c2*x0 - c3*x1) = exp(c1-c2*x0)*exp(-c3*x1)) but written to highlight that x1 acts as a log-scale shift of the transition midpoint. For concentration data spanning orders of magnitude, log-scale sensitivity is more natural. This is identical in structure to the current program but with different initial values emphasizing the log-scale x1 interpretation: c3 initial value set to 0.1 (instead of 0.001) to better handle concentration ranges. This is a targeted exploitation of the current best.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: datasets where x1 spans large concentration ranges (orders of magnitude)
active_paradigms.cautions: This is structurally identical to current program - the only change is initial_values for c3. May not improve if current fitting already finds good c3. The key question is whether the optimizer gets stuck at the wrong scale.

active_paradigms.idea: Three-parameter Gompertz without x1: y = c0*exp(-exp(c1 - c2*x0)) + c3, testing if x1 is truly informative
active_paradigms.description: Use the minimal Gompertz model y = c0*exp(-exp(c1 - c2*x0)) + c3 with only 4 constants and no x1 dependence. If x1 (concentration/parameter) is extracted as sequential indices for many datasets (non-numeric headers), it may be adding noise rather than signal. The parsimony penalty for 4 constants and ~15 nodes vs 6 constants and ~21 nodes would be significantly better. If the fit quality is comparable, the parsimony gain could improve combined_score. This tests the hypothesis that the shared structure across datasets is primarily temporal (x0) and x1 is dataset-specific noise. Initial values: c0=1.0, c1=3.0, c2=1e-3, c3=0.0.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: parsimony penalty reduction, robustness on datasets where x1 is non-informative sequential index
active_paradigms.cautions: If x1 genuinely captures concentration effects in many datasets, removing it will hurt fit quality significantly. The tradeoff between parsimony gain and fit loss needs to favor parsimony. For datasets where x1 is a real concentration, this model will underfit.
